title.zh: 竹瑾居 | 喜马拉雅艺术与亚洲古代艺术
title.en: Zhu Jin Ju | Himalayan Art and Asian Antiquities

description.zh: 竹瑾居专注于喜马拉雅艺术、藏传佛教艺术及相关亚洲古代艺术，面向藏家、机构、策展人与研究者提供收藏、展览与研究服务。
description.en: Zhu Jin Ju focuses on Himalayan art, Tibetan Buddhist art, and related Asian antiquities, serving collectors, institutions, curators, and researchers through collecting, exhibitions, and research.

homeIntro.zh: 竹瑾居关注喜马拉雅艺术、藏传佛教艺术及其与亚洲古代艺术之间的观看脉络，工作围绕收藏、研究、展览与图录展开。
homeIntro.en: Zhujinju focuses on Himalayan art, Tibetan Buddhist art, and their visual connections with the ancient arts of Asia, with work centered on collecting, research, exhibitions, and catalogues.

subtitle.zh: 工作围绕收藏、研究、展览与图录展开。
subtitle.en: The Work Is Structured Around Collecting, Research, Exhibitions, and Catalogues.

body.zh: 我们以作品本体为起点，在图像学、风格学、材质工艺与流传脉络之间建立判断，并通过展览、图录与研究文字持续公开这些判断。
body.en: We begin with the object itself, building judgement through iconography, style, material, craftsmanship, and provenance, and making that judgement public through exhibitions, catalogues, and research writing.

body.zh: 我们不以“海量上新”定义专业，也不将古代艺术处理为泛化的装饰品类。每一次展示、每一件作品与每一段文字，都应指向更准确的观看。
body.en: We do not define professionalism by constant new arrivals, nor reduce historical art to generalized decoration. Each display, object, and paragraph should lead to a more precise way of looking.

body.zh: 对竹瑾居而言，品牌并不独立于学术判断之外；网站、展览与图录，本身就是专业工作持续公开的一部分。
body.en: For Zhu Jin Ju, the brand is not separate from scholarly judgement; the website, exhibitions, and catalogues are themselves part of making that work public.

description.zh: 欢迎藏家、机构、策展人与我们联系。竹瑾居以作品咨询、借展洽谈、研究合作与图录交换为主要联络方向。
description.en: Collectors, institutions, curators, and researchers are welcome to get in touch regarding works, loans, research collaboration, and catalogue exchange.

aside.zh: 如咨询具体作品，可在表单中填写作品名称；若由作品页进入，意向作品将自动带入。成都会面采用预约制，可安排与我们看件。
aside.en: If you are inquiring about a specific work, include its title in the form. When arriving from an artwork page, the title is carried over automatically. Meetings in Chengdu are by appointment.

intro.zh: 关注喜马拉雅艺术、藏传佛教艺术及相关亚洲古代艺术，以作品、展览、图录与研究建立长期判断。
intro.en: Focused on Himalayan art, Tibetan Buddhist art, and related Asian antiquities, with long-term judgement built through objects, exhibitions, catalogues, and research.

collaborationNote.en: Collaboration and Loans Inquiries are typically addressed within 24 hours. We welcome requests regarding exhibition loans, research collaborations, catalogue exchanges, and private viewings by appointment.

intro.en: Focused on Himalayan art, Tibetan Buddhist art, and related Asian antiquities, with long-term judgement built through objects, exhibitions, catalogues, and research.

description.zh: 围绕当前正在进行的专题展览，继续呈现重点作品、图录整理与观看方法。
description.en: A current exhibition continuing Zhu Jin Ju's object-centered approach through selected works, catalogues, and ways of looking.

description.zh: 近期展览延续了竹瑾居以作品为核心的研究路径，相关图录与文章仍可继续索取与查阅。
description.en: A recent exhibition continuing Zhu Jin Ju's object-centered approach, with related catalogues and texts still available on request.

title.zh: 以作品为核心组织观看顺序
title.en: A Viewing Order Built Around the Object

description.zh: 优先呈现具有风格代表性、可比较性与观看张力的作品，版式始终服务于图像与器物本体。
description.en: Works of stylistic weight, comparative value, and visual tension are foregrounded, with the layout kept in service of the object.

title.zh: 围绕明确而长期的研究线索展开
title.en: Structured by Clear and Long-Term Research Lines

description.zh: 竹瑾居不追求门类堆叠，而是在少数真正重要的方向上持续积累判断、图像档案与展览经验。
description.en: Zhu Jin Ju does not pursue breadth for its own sake, but builds judgement, image archives, and exhibition experience within a few important fields.

title.zh: 由展览、图录与研究累积出的公开方法
title.en: A Public Method Built Through Exhibitions, Catalogues, and Research

description.zh: 对藏家、机构与研究者而言，可信赖并不来自夸张表达，而来自判断的一致性、公开资料的清晰度与持续积累。
description.en: For collectors, institutions, and researchers, trust comes not from spectacle but from consistent judgement, clear public documentation, and sustained work.

title.zh: 欢迎藏家、机构、策展人与研究者联系。
title.en: Collectors, Institutions, Curators, and Researchers Are Welcome.

description.zh: 如需咨询具体作品、专题合作、机构借展、图录交换或研究交流，可通过联系页面提交信息。
description.en: For inquiries about individual works, collaborations, institutional loans, catalogue exchange, or research discussion, please use the contact page.

description.zh: 竹瑾居是一家专注于喜马拉雅艺术、藏传佛教艺术及相关亚洲古代艺术的品牌，服务对象包括藏家、博物馆、机构、策展人与研究者。
description.en: Zhu Jin Ju is dedicated to Himalayan art, Tibetan Buddhist art, and related Asian antiquities, serving collectors, museums, institutions, curators, and researchers.

aside.zh: 我们相信，真正可靠的古代艺术网站，不应以话术主导，而应以判断、结构与节制建立信任。
aside.en: A reliable site for historical art should be led not by rhetoric, but by judgement, structure, and restraint.

aside.en: The entries follow the information structure common to dealership and research catalogues, allowing browsing to move naturally into comparison and inquiry.

description.en: Exhibitions and catalogues are not supplementary gestures but part of the core practice itself. This page places both together to show continuity in research output.

aside.en: Texts are organized around ways of looking, object judgement, and curatorial practice so that articles, exhibitions, and works can be read in relation.

statement.zh: 竹瑾居关注喜马拉雅艺术、藏传佛教艺术及其与亚洲古代艺术之间的观看脉络，工作围绕收藏、研究、展览与图录展开。
statement.en: Zhu Jin Ju focuses on Himalayan art, Tibetan Buddhist art, and their broader dialogues with Asian antiquities, with work structured around collecting, research, exhibitions, and catalogues.

about.zh: 我们以作品本体为起点，在图像学、风格学、材质工艺与流传脉络之间建立判断，并通过展览、图录与研究文字持续公开这些判断。
about.en: We begin with the object itself, building judgement through iconography, style, material, craftsmanship, and provenance, and making that judgement public through exhibitions, catalogues, and research writing.

methodology.zh: 以作品为中心，尊重图像与器物自身的时间性。
methodology.en: Place the object at the center, respecting the temporal depth held within images and ritual forms.

methodology.zh: 以研究支撑判断，避免将古代艺术降格为单纯装饰消费。
methodology.en: Ground judgement in research, rather than reducing historical art to decorative consumption.

methodology.zh: 以长期主义组织内容，持续积累展览、图录与学术输出。
methodology.en: Organize content with a long-term view, steadily building exhibitions, catalogues, and scholarly output.

description.zh: 聚焦早期西藏、尼泊尔、印度与相关区域的佛教造像。
description.en: Focusing on early Buddhist sculpture from Tibet, Nepal, India, and related regions.

description.zh: 关注图像系统完整、设色沉着、传承清晰的佛教绘画。
description.en: Works selected for coherent iconographic systems, restrained color, and clear lines of transmission.

description.zh: 包括卷轴、纸本与跨区域图像传统中的重要作品。
description.en: Including scrolls, works on paper, and important paintings across intersecting visual traditions.

description.zh: 涵盖供器、法器与兼具宗教性与工艺价值的收藏方向。
description.en: Covering offering vessels, liturgical implements, and objects valued for both ritual and material significance.

description.zh: 侧重尼瓦尔工匠体系与喜马拉雅区域风格流动。
description.en: With particular attention to Newar craftsmanship and the movement of style across the Himalayas.

description.zh: 已公开的展览项目围绕观看方式、材质判断与作品比较展开。
description.en: Published exhibition projects structured around ways of seeing, material judgement, and comparison.

description.zh: 图录以作品信息、页码索引与研究摘要构成稳定的引用基础。
description.en: Catalogues provide stable reference through object entries, pagination, and research summaries.

description.zh: 围绕观看方法、展览方法与市场判断持续发布研究与札记。
description.en: Research notes continue to address methods of looking, exhibition practice, and market judgement.

viewingNote.zh: 帝释天，一位佛教里从古印度神话中继承而来的天神之一。佛经里常说，祂会在佛陀出生、成道或涅槃时出现，礼敬佛陀、守护僧团。祂是忉利天的主宰，居住在须弥山顶，所以祂的形象往往象征着威仪、护法和天界的秩序。 须弥山，在佛教、印度教、耆那教等传统里，被看作是宇宙的中心。山顶是忉利天，帝释天的居所就在其中，统领着三十三天神；四面则由四大天王守护人间。须弥山原本并不是现实中的山，而是一种宇宙的象征 。 而在现实里，西藏阿里的冈仁波齐，被认为是须弥山在人间的显现，是佛教、印度教、耆那教和苯教共同尊崇的圣地 。印度教说那是湿婆的居所；藏传佛教认为它是曼荼罗的核心，同样也是帝释天的居所；苯教则称它是宇宙的“九层天柱”。所以，冈仁波齐成了人们可见 、可亲近的“宇宙中心”，朝圣绕山，其实就是在与宇宙的轴心建立身体性的连接。 佛教的世界观里，有三界：欲界、色界和无色界。欲界是我们熟悉的人、阿修罗和欲界天，充满欲望与追逐 。色界是通过禅定才能进入的境界，超越了粗重的欲望，但依然保有“色身”，也就是一种清净的形态 。无色界则是没有形体，只有心识 。而忉利天就属于色界的最高层之一，由帝释天主宰。所谓“色身”，可以理解为一种带着“烟火气”的存在 。天人虽然超越了欲望，但他们依然有宫殿、有花园、有 天乐与清风 ，祂们不是抽象到无法触摸的概念，而是带着气息的生命。这就像一种超脱后的余温，提醒我们：真正的超越，并不是彻底远离，而是以更清澈、更轻盈的方式继续存在 。我们眼前这尊帝释天的造像，正是匠人在对佛教文化理解后赋予的形象化表达，让这种抽象的存在，变得具体可感 。 我们眼前看到的这尊帝释天造像，第一眼会让人觉得特别轻盈，甚至有一点飘逸的味道 。祂以一种很放松的姿态坐在莲台上，一手结说法印，另一手则握着金刚杵撑住莲台。金刚杵在佛教里有很重要的象征意义，它代表着不可摧毁的佛法和神力。换句话说，它既是守护的武器，也是智慧和权威的象征 。 如果仔细观察祂的姿态，你会发现整个人的体态非常挺拔 。那只结印的手臂自然地倚靠在大腿之上，既放松又带着一种空间上的张力。祂并不是紧绷地撑在那里，而是像呼吸一样自然，就像一个人随意坐下，却依然显得有力量、有气度 。这种“不用力”的从容，恰恰传达出帝释天自在的神性 。 这尊造像出自马拉王朝早期，和后期一些厚重大块面的雕塑不同，这件作品的体块并不强调厚重感，而是偏向一种更灵动的塑造方式 。匠人没有用粗犷的体量去堆砌力量，而是通过肌肉的紧实感，表现出一种健康、充盈的生命力。那种力量，不是压迫式的，而是一种由内而外散发的美感 。因为佛教造像的体块感往往会随着题材的不同而调整，帝释天作为天界的主神，祂不需要像护法神那样厚重和威严，而是更强调色界天人的轻灵和自在 。于是，这里我们看到的，不是沉重的压迫，而是一种“若有若无”的体量感，让祂看起来仿佛带着空气般的仙气 。观者的目光在祂的身体周围可以自由游走，不会被某个厚重的部分卡住，这正是“空灵意味”在造型上的体现 。 帝释天头戴高耸 、华丽的宝冠，微微侧首，耳边的带饰和耳环好像随风在飘动，这让静止的雕塑多了一份流动感 。祂的脸庞圆润、柔和，带着马拉王朝时期特有的甜美和亲切。虽然祂是天界的主神，但这种甜美感拉近了与人之间的距离，让祂少了一些高高在上的威严，而多了一份慈和与雅致 。 帝释天身体的塑造同样耐人寻味 。上半身呈倒三角形，肩膀宽阔、结实，从腋下往腰部内收的线条自然，随着微微的侧首，整个身体也出现了一个轻柔的弯曲。这种线条让造像形态显得灵动，而不是僵硬的对称，是一种有节奏、有呼吸的动态 。腰间的衣结立体而细致，胸前的禅修带、腿上的飘带，都柔软地弯曲着，这些细节也都为整体增添了一种灵动的气息 。可以说，这件作品在造型上既写实又有理想化的提升。祂符合人体的姿态规律，看上去非常自然，但同时又通过比例和细节让整个形象超越现实。这种塑造使祂传达出一种“没有被任何东西拘束”的感觉，好像祂真的坐在空中，而不是被固定在石头里 。 我常喜欢用“意味”两个字来形容一尊雕塑所散发的气息 。在雕塑的语境里，意味不是单纯的外表美，而是由形态、姿态、比例、材质、空间关系乃至气质共同营造出来的一种精神氛围。它像空气一样，看不见，但你靠近时就能感觉到 。那是一种超越了物质的精神性和思想性 。你可能会在不知不觉中屏住呼吸，因为你感受到的不仅仅是一个神祇的形象，而是一种被氛围裹挟的体验 。 这尊帝释天的意味 ，是空灵的。祂轻盈，没有压迫感，祂的存在更像是一阵风 、一抹光，自在地停驻在空间里 。这种轻盈不仅仅是形式上的，它还象征着超脱 ，祂超越了世俗的沉重，超越了人间的束缚，展现出天界神祇独有的自由与自在 。 当我们站在祂面前时，这种空灵意味会慢慢感染我们 。你会觉得自己的心也轻了，呼吸也柔和了。祂不是用威严去震慑你，而是用一种若即若离的温柔气息，把你带入一种宁静和自在的境界。这，也是这件帝释天最动人的地方——它不仅仅只是冰冷的金属元素塑成的形象，更是一种能与观者心灵相通的精神空间。
viewingNote.en: Indra belongs to one of the deities in Buddhism, inherited from ancient Indian mythology. Buddhist scriptures often mention that he appears at the birth, enlightenment, or nirvana of the Buddha, paying homage to the Buddha and protecting the monastic community. As the ruler of the Trāyastriṃśa heaven, he resides atop Mount Sumeru, so his imagery often symbolizes dignity, dharma protection, and the order of the celestial realm. In religions such as Buddhism, Hinduism, and Jainism, Mount Sumeru is regarded as the center of the universe. At its summit lies the Trāyastriṃśa heaven, where Indra resides, ruling over the thirty-three celestial gods. The four sides of the mountain are guarded by the Four Heavenly Kings, who protect the human world. Mount Sumeru is not a physical mountain but rather a cosmic symbol. In the physical world, Mount Kailash in Ngari, Tibet, is considered the earthly manifestation of Mount Sumeru and is revered as a sacred site by Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Bon. Hinduism describes it as the abode of Shiva; Tibetan Buddhism regards it as the core of the mandala and also the residence of Indra; Bon tradition refers to it as the "nine-layered celestial pillar" of the universe. Thus, Mount Kailash has become a visible and accessible "cosmic center" for people. Pilgrims circumambulate the mountain, establishing a physical connection with the axis of the universe. In the Buddhist worldview, there are three realms: the desire realm, the form realm, and the formless realm. The desire realm includes the familiar humans, asuras, and desire realm heavens, filled with desires and pursuits. The form realm is a state accessible only through meditation, transcending coarse desires but still retaining a "form body," a purified manifestation. The formless realm has no physical form, only consciousness. The Trāyastriṃśa heaven belongs to the highest layers of the form realm, ruled by Indra. The so-called "form body" can be understood as an existence with a "worldly aura." Although celestial beings have transcended desires, they still possess palaces, gardens, celestial music, and gentle breezes. They are not abstract, untouchable concepts but living beings with vitality. This is like the residual warmth after transcendence, reminding us that true transcendence is not about complete detachment but about continuing to exist in a clearer and lighter way. The statue of Indra before us is precisely the artisan's visual expression, informed by an understanding of Buddhist culture, making this abstract existence tangible and perceptible. At first glance, this statue of Indra appears exceptionally light, even somewhat ethereal. He sits on a lotus throne in a relaxed posture, one hand forming the teaching mudra (vitarka mudra), while the other holds a vajra, resting it on the lotus throne. The vajra holds significant symbolic meaning in Buddhism, representing the indestructible nature of the Dharma and divine power. In other words, it is both a protective weapon and a symbol of wisdom and authority. If you observe his posture closely, you will notice that his entire body is very upright. The arm forming the mudra rests naturally on his thigh, both relaxed and possessing a spatial tension. He is not rigidly propped up but appears to be naturally breathing, like a person sitting casually yet still exuding strength and grace. This effortless composure precisely conveys Indra's self-existent divinity. This sculpture dates back to the early Malla Dynasty. Unlike some later sculptures that emphasize heaviness and block-like mass, this piece does not focus on the sense of weight but leans toward a more dynamic modeling approach. The artisan did not use rough volumes to accumulate power but instead expressed a healthy, brimming vitality through the firmness of the muscles. Such strength doesn’t signal oppression but a beauty that radiates from within. Because the sense of volume in Buddhist sculptures often adjusts according to the subject, Indra, as a celestial deity, is not attributed the heaviness and majesty of a protector deity but rather the lightness and ease of form realm beings. Thus, what we see here is not heavy oppression but a sense of volume that is "almost there yet not," making him appear as if surrounded by an airy, celestial aura. The viewer's gaze can freely wander around his body without being caught by anything protruding. This is precisely the embodiment of ethereal essence in form. Indra wears a tall, ornate crown, his head slightly tilted, with the ribbons and earrings near his ears seemingly fluttering in the wind, adding a sense of flow to the static sculpture. His face is round and soft, carrying the sweetness and approachability characteristic of the Malla period. Although he is a celestial deity, this sweetness bridges the distance with humans, reducing his lofty majesty and adding a touch of benevolence and elegance. The modeling of Indra's body is equally intriguing. His upper body forms an inverted triangle, with broad, solid shoulders and a natural inward taper from the armpits to the waist. With his slight head tilt, his entire body exhibits a gentle curve. These lines make the statue appear dynamic rather than rigidly symmetrical, creating a rhythmic, breathing movement. The knot of his garment at the waist is three-dimensional and detailed, while the meditation belt across his chest and the ribbons on his legs curve softly, adding to the overall sense of liveliness. It can be said that this work is both realistic and ideally elevated in form. It conforms to the laws of human posture, appearing very natural, yet simultaneously transcends reality through proportion and detail. This modeling conveys a sense of being "unconstrained by anything," as if he is truly sitting in the air rather than fixed in stone. I often like to use the term yìwèi (essence or aura) to describe the atmosphere exuded by a sculpture. In the context of sculpture, yìwèi is not merely external beauty but a spiritual ambiance created collectively by form, posture, proportion, material, spatial relationships, and even temperament. It is like air—invisible but palpable. It is a spirituality and ideality that transcends the material. You might find yourself holding your breath unconsciously because what you perceive is not just the image of a deity but an experience enveloped by an atmosphere. The yìwèi of this Indra is ethereal. He is light, without any sense of oppression; his presence is like a gust of wind or a streak of light, resting at ease in space. This lightness I write of is not restricted to form but extends to its spiritual transcendence. He rises above the heaviness of the mundane world and the constraints of the human realm, showcasing the unique freedom and ease of celestial deities. When we stand before him, this ethereal essence slowly affects us. You may feel your heart lighten and your breath soften. He does not awe you with majesty but draws you into a state of tranquility and ease with a gentle, elusive aura. This is also the most touching aspect of this Indra—it is not just an image formed from cold metallic elements but a spiritual space that can connect with the viewer's soul.

label.zh: John Adam Stainton（1921-1991）舊藏， 贈予Aberdeen and Hindus六世伯爵及伯爵夫人為禮物，之後由其後人繼承。
label.en: Formerly in the collection of John Adam Stainton (1921–1991), gifted to the 6th Earl and Countess of Aberdeen, later inherited by their descendants.

viewingNote.zh: 吉尔吉特地区位于今天巴基斯坦的吉尔吉特 - 巴尔蒂斯坦地区，地处喀喇 昆仑山脉和兴都库什山脉之间，历史上是连接印度、喀喇汗、巴克特里亚等地的 重要通道，为丝绸之路的一个分支，也是佛教在中亚与南亚之间的重要传播站。 贵霜王朝大约在公元 1 世纪至 3 世纪统治了中亚和南亚的广大地区，其疆 域包括今天的阿富汗、巴基斯坦北部 、印度西北部，以及塔吉克斯坦部分地区 。 曾作为贵霜版图一部分的吉尔吉特，在约公元 5 世纪贵霜王朝衰落之后，于当 地逐渐发展成了独立的王国。此时期的吉尔吉特与克什米尔河谷、乌仑、吐蕃 等政权交往密切，时有冲突、时有联盟 。在公元 6–8 世纪吉尔吉特王国迎来了 鼎峰时期，发展出了自己的统治体系，以及扩建造了宫殿与佛教寺院 。自 8 世 纪末开始，吉尔吉特王国就开始逐渐被吐蕃、克什米尔王国等势力争夺。最终 在 11 世纪左右，被克什米尔的洛哈拉王朝并入，王国彻底终结。由于地缘关 系，吉尔吉特的艺术风格融合了犍陀罗风格、笈多风格与克什米尔风格。尤其 是吉尔吉特王国与克什米尔王国曾相邻，又都是佛法兴盛之地，两地交流频 繁，制作的造像艺术风格具有相似性 。如今两地又都位于大克什米尔地区，所 以现在喜马拉雅艺术学界，则将这两个地方的造像统称为克什米尔艺术风格。 大克什米尔地区作为整个佛教脉络中一个极为重要的存在，幸运的是，由 于曾与西藏的交流和来往间，少量作品能有幸在西藏地区得到了保护和延续，成 为了一笔极为珍贵的人类文化遗产。如此件吉尔吉特说法弥勒造像，已知存留 下来的同题材，据统计，在各大公私馆藏中的数量屈指可数，在佛教古美术 中，这件 8 世纪的说法弥勒造像为一件重要且稀缺的作品 。 做一件佛教雕塑，比例的成败是决定这件作品会不会成功的重要因素。同 样，一件佛教雕塑的比例、大小也取决于要放置位置的高低 。当看到这件作品 时，我的第一反应是匠人将弥勒坐姿的比例把握的非常好，祂诞生的初衷构 思，应该也不是想放置于很远的高处，而是当虔诚的人走过时微微抬头时的目 光所及之处 。放置这样的高度可能是为了让路过之人能更容易的察觉到“大小 之辩”所带来的思索，以及在每个目光碰撞间的发问：“身为弥勒的我既然象征 着未来，那您觉得时间存在吗？” 弥勒整个头部的塑造从球体的基础上展开，头型椭圆，螺发颗颗饱满，像 圆形般有序排列，这种发型样式带有后犍陀罗的遗风 。小巧的脸庞，呈现出向 内收的弧形，存在感很强的五官，量感塑造得非常大，极具冲击力。深刻线的 眉毛下是细长如鱼腹般的眼睛，眉心中上方有绿松石点缀（此种镶嵌方式笔者 推测应为造像在西藏传世、供奉期间所嵌，示意白毫），宽厚的鼻部下方为清 晰、聚拢的嘴部，由于脸部整体也是采用一个像球体般椭圆的脸型，所以匠人 在下巴的塑造上，也采用了一个圆形臌胀的形式来增强面部整体的柔和及饱满 度 。不论于正面还是侧面欣赏时，弱化骨骼的塑造突出了一种圆润、丰盈的状 态，使得脸部转折既柔和又含蓄。但由于弥勒脸部刻线清晰、深刻，所以脸部 也依旧不失一种锐利、凌厉的感觉 。当我们离远了观看时，弥勒散发出的精神 气质非常强烈，是一种柔中带刚的状态 。 每当欣赏一尊雕塑时，我会习惯性的从不同角度去感受祂所散发的情绪张 力。不经意间，当我从侧面与其眼神相对时，弧形上扬且略微俯视前方的眼神 使我内心生一种矛盾的情绪——既慈悲、又冷漠 。过了数日，当我再次去观看 时，忽然意识到，这种复杂情绪的感知，既来自造像本身的艺术特征，也来自 于不同空间、不同心境下内心与意识的投射。 祂似乎在关心着一切，又仿佛对一切无动于衷，让我们感到既敬畏又亲 近 。也许，当内心正感到孤独，从造像中看到的可能是“冷漠”；当内心正在寻 求安慰，在造像的微笑中看到可能就是“慈悲”；当你我同时渴望理解与自由，那 这种“慈悲而超然”的神情也许就会深深触动你我 。宁静即是超越！看似无 情，实则悲心深藏；看似疏远，实则无所不包 。正因祂知道一切是空，正因为祂早已把你从苦中看透，却不评判你——这是最温暖的慈悲，祂的慈悲，于万象 之中，依然为你我保留觉醒的可能 。 站在祂面前，不知为何久久移不开目光 。弥勒手结说法印，自然下垂的通肩 式袈裟上半身呈放射状的褶皱，下半身则宛如湿衣裹体 。虽然吉尔吉特的造像 脸庞与写实的犍陀罗有明显区别，但从在袈裟的褶皱与服饰结构上仍能看到清 晰继承 。而服饰两侧的衣褶花边富有柔软的动态感，体现着贵族化与神圣化 。 微微带点坡度的大腿，和衣服自然向内折叠的纵深感，凸显着腿部的坐姿，使得 坐姿看起来既稳固又不呆板，周身似乎围绕着一股盘旋上升的气息 。弥勒椅座 在由软垫和建筑结构构成的底座之上，软垫上刻画一朵朵融合了波斯风格的小 花 。由罗马柱形制支撑的台座内站立着两只圆雕狮子，此种结构受希腊、罗马风 格影响明显，为吉尔吉很具代表性的风格特点之一。作为弥勒坐骑的狮子肌肉 突出，身形不仅稳健 、有力，还带着一种憨态、粗犷的张力之美 。头尾直逼天花 板的硕大身躯占满了两侧的建筑空间，似乎只要一起身整个世界就会地动山摇。 此种建筑结构和狮子体积感的塑造，在视觉上凸显着弥勒的庞大，而下方宽 大、具有典型时代特征的莲瓣将一切托举。底部已有装藏的内堂与镶嵌的绿松 石同因，于西藏传世供奉期间而装 。 如一座山、一片星辰般宏大，端正庄严的身姿如同置身于宇宙之中。这种由视 觉神经所带的画面让人不禁思考——宇宙之大、令人沉默；人之渺小、却诞生伟大。 作为碳基生命的我们，由于物种的不同，在星球上岁感受到的时间长度也有 所不同。海龟的生命周期大约一百五十年左右、大象大约九十年左右、人类大约 七十年左右、猫大约十五年左右、蝴蝶大约十一个月左右、而浮游的生命周期则 是一天 。在宇宙目前 138 亿年的时间周期面前，我们看似或快、或慢的一生只 是一瞬间而已；在辽阔的星空中，我们赖以生存的星球也只是旅行者一号回头 时看到的那个暗淡蓝点而已 。尽管如此，但渺小的我们在这星球上却又是那么 的独特和伟大 。如果以“类”的形式来划分物种，我们“人类”所特有的抽象思 维、推理能力以及创造能力，让我们拥有了不一样的情感 、智慧 。如康德曾 言：“有两种东西，我愈是经常而持久地思考它们，它们在我心中唤起的惊叹和 敬畏就愈加新鲜和不断增强：一是我头顶上浩瀚的星空，一是我心中高尚的道 德律 。”作为一个拥有自由意志的个体，我们内心却对道德达成了一种无条件的 义务共识 。善良、诚实、宽容、尊重等等这些行为，不是因为它对我们有利，我 们才应当去做的，而是思维中的理性本身所自带的。即使在艰难的处境中，人依 然有对善负责的能力，这种内在的道德尊严令人震撼 。身为碳基生命，在空间而 言，我们最终的归宿都是‘化作春泥’，这是自然规律，也是生命的循环。 于时间而言，就如与弥勒对视时祂的‘发问’一样——时间存在吗？在日常 生活中，我们看着月升日落 、钟表滴答作响、自己从孩童即将步入到中年，这一切的体验都让我们感觉着时间真实的流动性 。牛顿曾说：“时间是绝对的。时 间像一条独立的直线 ，与空间无关，无论你是否在动，它都匀速流逝”。爱因斯 坦曾说：“时间是相对的。时间并非恒定，它和空间是交织的，组成“时空”。如 果你靠近光速，或处在强引力场中，时间会变慢，它取决于观察者的位置和运 动状态”。当代表量子引力的理论出现时，有些物理学家提出：“时间可能并不存 在，它只是我们用来描述变化的一种幻觉或方便的语言工具”。而佛教则并不认 为时间是“实有”的，它是无常与缘起的一部分。过去、现在、未来都在不断生 灭之中，当下这一念才是我们真正拥有的。其实，不论时间存在与否，“未来” 由当下的每一刻构成，也许彼岸就是当下。而此刻正在思考这个问题的我与正 翻看到书籍此段落的你，才是真实可触摸的，也许对于这一开放性问题的思考也 正是当下、也正是每个目光碰撞间，身为未来佛的弥勒送给我们的礼物吧 ...
viewingNote.en: This 8th-century sculpture of Maitreya in the dharma teaching gesture is a significant and rare masterpiece in the realm of ancient Buddhist art. Gilgit, located in present-day Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan, lies between the Karakoram and Hindu Kush mountain ranges. Historically, it served as a vital corridor connecting India, the Karakhanid territories, Bactria, and beyond—a branch of the Silk Road and a crucial hub for the spread of Buddhism, particularly Mahayana, between Central and South Asia. The Kushan Empire (approx. 1st–3rd century CE) once ruled vast swathes of Central and South Asia, encompassing modern-day Afghanistan, northern Pakistan, northwestern India, and parts of Tajikistan. Gilgit, once part of the Kushan realm, evolved into an independent kingdom around the 5th century CE after the empire's decline. During this period, Gilgit maintained close ties—alternating between conflict and alliance—with the Kashmir Valley, Uddiyana, and Tibetan polities. The 6th–8th centuries marked Gilgit's golden age, as it developed its own governance system and expanded palaces and Buddhist monasteries. By the late 8th century, however, the kingdom became a contested prize among Tibetan and Kashmiri forces, eventually being absorbed by Kashmir's Lohara Dynasty in the 11th century, marking its end. Due to its geographic position, Gilgit's artistic style blended Gandharan, Gupta, and Kashmiri influences. Its proximity to Kashmir—both being flourishing centers of Buddhism—resulted in frequent exchanges and shared artistic traits. Today, scholars of Himalayan art collectively classify the sculptural styles of these two regions as "Kashmiri." As a pivotal nexus in Buddhist history, the greater Kashmir region fortunately preserved a handful of artworks through its exchanges with Tibet, leaving behind an invaluable cultural legacy. This Gilgit Preaching Maitreya is a prime example. Extant works of this subject are exceedingly rare in global collections, with most being Tibetan productions. Authentic, aesthetically refined pieces from greater Kashmir that remain accessible are even scarcer.Proportion, in Buddhist sculptures, is a crucial factor for its success. The intended viewing height also influences its dimensions. Here, Maitreya’s seated posture is masterfully balanced, suggesting it was designed not for distant elevation but for an intimate encounter—where a devotee, upon slightly raising their gaze, would meet the deity’s eyes. This deliberate placement invites contemplation on the nature of scale and prompts a silent dialogue: "As Maitreya, the symbol of the future, I ask: do you believe time exists?" Maitreya’s head is sculpted from an oval base, with perfectly rounded snail-shell curls arranged in orderly rows—a stylistic legacy of late Gandharan art. The compact face curves gently inward, while the pronounced, oversized features command attention. Beneath sharply incised brows are slender, fish-belly-like eyes, and a turquoise inlay (likely added during the statue’s later preservation in Tibet, symbolizing the urna) accents the forehead above the nose. The full lips are finely rendered, and the rounded chin enhances the face’s softness and volume. Viewed from any angle, the subdued bone structure emphasizes a plump, serene vitality, yet the sharp facial lines retain a piercing intensity. From a distance, Maitreya exudes a potent spiritual presence—gentle yet resolute. When studying a sculpture, I habitually observe it from multiple angles to sense its emotional resonance. Once, glimpsing its profile, the slightly upturned, downward-gazing eyes evoked a paradoxical blend of compassion and detachment. Days later, revisiting the piece, I realized this duality stemmed not only from the statue’s artistry but also from the interplay of space and the viewer’s psyche. Maitreya seems to care deeply yet remain aloof—simultaneously awe-inspiring and intimate. In solitude, one might perceive "indifference” and in seeking solace, the same expression may seem to radiate "compassion”. When yearning for understanding and freedom, that "compassionate transcendence" might then again strike a profound chord. Serenity is transcendence! What seems indifferent is, in truth, deeply merciful; what seems distant embraces all. Knowing all is emptiness, seeing through suffering yet refraining from judgment—this is the warmest compassion, preserving the possibility of awakening for every being.Standing before it, I found myself transfixed. Maitreya’s hands form the teaching mudra (dharma chakra mudra), while the sanghati robe drapes naturally, its upper folds radiating outward and the lower clinging like wet fabric. Though Gilgit’s facial style diverges from Gandharan realism, the robe’s pleats and structure clearly inherit its legacy. The delicate, dynamic hem and aristocratic drapery convey divinity. The subtly sloping thighs and deep, inward folds of the robe stabilize the posture without rigidity, as if suffused with a spiraling energy. The figure sits atop a cushioned, architectonic base adorned with Persian-inspired florets. The throne, supported by Roman-style columns, features two sculpted lions—a Greco-Roman influence distinctive to Gilgit. As Maitreya’s mounts, the lions are robust and muscular, their charm and raw power amplifying the deity’s majesty. Their colossal forms, nearly filling the architectural niche, suggest seismic force if stirred. This composition visually magnifies Maitreya’s grandeur, underscored by the broad, period-typical lotus petals below. The sealed relic cavity and turquoise inlays were likely added during the statue’s Tibetan sojourn. Imagine shrinking oneself and gazing up at this statue of Maitreya; he shall loom like a mountain or a constellation above you, evoking the cosmos with his dignified posture. Such a vision compels one to ponder—the vastness of the universe mesmerizes us, yet in our insignificance, greatness is born. As carbon-based life forms, our perception of time varies by species: ~150 years for turtles, ~90 for elephants, ~70 for humans, ~15 for cats, ~11 months for butterflies, and a mere day for mayflies. But compared to the universe’s 13.8 billion years, these lives perceived as "long" or "short" are but a blink. In the cosmic expanse, our planet is but the "pale blue dot" glimpsed by Voyager 1. Yet, within this fragility lies our uniqueness. As “Homo sapiens”, our abstract thought, reasoning, and creativity grant us extraordinary emotions and wisdom. As Kant noted: "Two things fill the mind with ever-increasing wonder and awe: the starry heavens above me and the moral law within me." Despite free will, we share an unconditional consensus on moral duty—kindness, honesty, tolerance, and respect are not mere utilities but emanations of rational thought itself. Even in adversity, humans uphold moral dignity, a testament to our nobility. As carbon-based life, our ultimate fate in the grand scheme of space is to “return to the earth as spring soil”— this is the law of nature and the cycle of life. Pondering further upon the question raised by the gaze with Maitreya: Does time exist?. We witness moons rising, clocks ticking, and our own aging, all reinforcing time’s flow. Newton declared time to be absolute—a uniform river. Einstein relativized it, entwining it with space. Quantum gravity theorists now suggest time may be illusory, a linguistic tool for change. Buddhism, too, denies time’s inherent reality, framing it as impermanent and interdependent. Past, present, and future ceaselessly arise and dissolve; only the present moment is truly ours. Whether time exists or not, the "future" is woven from now. Perhaps the “other shore” is this shore. Perhaps I, pondering upon these thoughts, and you, reading these words, are the tangible reality. Perhaps the contemplation of this open-ended question is itself a gift bestowed upon us by Maitreya, the Future Buddha, in this very moment—in every exchange of glances…

viewingNote.zh: 每次看到那些富有积淀和厚重感的色彩，我都不由自主的被它们所吸引。从 深邃的蓝天到红、白、黑相间的建筑群，自然纯净与艳丽的色彩潜藏着吸引人们 的奥秘，除了是一种感官的享受，心中更是感到一种深深的敬畏之情。这片土地 让人着迷，我们似乎对这片土地一点都不陌生，反而给人们带来一种愉悦和一种 深刻的熟悉感；在原始文明社会，那些充满自然美、色彩美的地方正是能够提高 人类祖先生存机会的地方，人类对于色彩常常是带着求生的内涵，比如，红色常 常被用于象征火和热情，也是对于危险与祭祀的刺激。与此相反，绿色因为与树 木和水源有深刻的联结，被视为和平和生命的色彩。在这片土地上所展现给我 们的色彩、线条正好与人类思维和情感相互关联，让我们与这天地间的一切产生 了联系。那与内心意识深处产生的原始情感共振，也使这里的每一个角落都充 满了神秘与浩瀚。 在中国西藏，由于其特殊的地理环境和气候条件，对植物的生存构成了一定 的挑战。于是生命就在这短短的时间里，努力张扬出更加蓬勃旺盛的力量，生命 借由色彩都努力地绽放，显现出无与伦比的热烈、与奔放。自然风景和深厚的文 化传统造就了独特的西藏艺术，它将自然和文化融合，展现出令人惊叹的美感和 视觉冲击力，在灵性与原始本性的底色上传递着一种精神之美。当这些色彩反照 应到一件件艺术品上时，历史与文化的脉络似乎也变得隐约可见。 公元 841 年，吐蕃赞普朗达玛发起了灭佛运动，导致西藏的佛教艺术历经了 一百多年的空白期，部分僧人纷纷逃到了安多地区（即现在青海一带）融入了当 地的佛教氛围中，在这一百多年间，青海逐渐发展成了一个新的佛教中心。于此 期间吐蕃王室的子孙们也分别去到阿里地区建立了自己的王朝，直到 978 年，佛 教才再次从青海和阿里两路传入卫藏地区，就分别形成了上路弘法和下路弘法的 历史背景。公元 10 世纪，声名显赫的古格王朝统治者崇尚佛教，第三代国王意 希沃尤为突出，他提倡和引进佛教，成为了古格王朝历史上最著名的佛教护持 者。他曾派遣家族中 21 人赴克什米尔等地求法，其中一员名叫仁钦桑布的僧人 学成归来后，成为了西藏佛教后弘初期著名的译经大师。学成归国的仁钦桑布专门邀请了 32 位克什米尔匠人来到古格，帮助建寺和造像，于是藏西早期的艺术 风格以克什米尔为主。11 世纪随着印度高僧达磨波罗和阿底峡入藏途经阿里、及 卫藏地区弘法，帕拉美学元素也因此传入高原腹地。此时随着佛教在西藏的传播 及日渐强盛的影响力，流行于卫藏地区的帕拉风格绘画也被传到以克什米尔风格 为主的西藏西部，并影响和融入到了其艺术创作中。时间发展到 13 世纪，随着 尼泊尔风潮的席卷，不同地区将不同美学元素相融，创作出了独具地方特色的艺 术作品。此幅噶当派上师对坐绘画，则出自十三世纪西藏西部，将帕拉美学元素 及尼泊尔美学元素相融合的典型之作。 上师题材作为藏传佛教中极具特点的艺术门类，不仅只存在于三维雕塑 中，匠人们也同样通过二维平面上的色彩、线条和构图来呈现着那一位位真实存 在过的人物。上师作为佛和众生之间的桥梁，享有崇高的地位。在上师圆寂 后，弟子们会根据其生前模样为其塑像、绘画，以表纪念。此幅以人物为主题的 肖像画，为噶当派上师对坐，唐卡以分段叙事、棋格式布局以及突出主题的构图 形式，描述着两位上师在修行中交流与对谈的画面。 画面缓缓展开，首先映入眼帘的是一片宁静而庄严的氛围。以红色调为主的 画面，在第一眼见时，则被那扑面而的鲜活气息所吸引。画面上段以棋格构图的 方式分别绘有三排神灵和喇嘛，佛、菩萨、瑜伽士、喇嘛、供养人的形象共聚一 堂。这些形象则分别代表着不同的境界和力量，不仅展现了佛教信仰的完整性和 丰富性，也共同编织着一段关于传承、慈悲与智慧的故事。随着视线往下顺 延，半圆形的背光上方岩洞内分别居住着带噶当派僧帽的祖师（或阿底峡尊者）、护 法、佛、菩萨、大成就等十位不同人物。其中具有典型元代风格的胜乐金刚和金 刚手菩萨，形象浑圆壮实，胖乎乎的体态不禁让人升起喜悦之心；其他祖师、菩 萨类形象同样分别手持不同法器、结不同手印，画工细腻间象征着不同的佛教教 义。其中古铜色肌肤的大成就者帕当巴桑杰，以其经典的形象出现在画面右 侧，如铜铃般圆瞪的神情炯炯澄澈、纯真灵动。帕当巴桑杰肖像的描绘与西藏西 部古格帕尔洞窟的一幅 12 世纪壁画上的形象相似构图上，唐卡巧妙地运用了空间分割，将两位上师置于画面的中段位置，以 突出主题。艾米 · 海勒在 2008 的文章《噶举派两位藏族上师的唐卡》中认为此 幅唐卡为噶举派传承，虽然没有铭文可以非常准确的确认其身份，但通过右侧上 师的俗家形象及其在其他绘画中的五官和形象特征分析，笔者认为应该为噶当派 创始人仲敦巴的可能性更大，画面左侧的人物肖像应该为仲敦巴的老师或者上 师。两位上师分别结跏趺坐安坐于带六拏具背光的仰覆式莲台之上。六拏具上 方摩羯的尾部变化成卷草，旋转的卷草细腻、灵动，表现出尼泊尔美学元素的渗 透。尽管上师造像是根据其生前模样、特征进行描绘，但眉眼间的神情、线条和 绘画笔触透着明显的帕拉绘画风格神韵。 上师的衣饰上点缀着模仿金线刺绣的花卉图案，繁复的花卉在线条的穿插 勾勒下繁而不乱、节奏明确，色彩鲜艳且不失庄重，一笔一画间都蕴含着匠人的 虔诚与敬意。上师面容慈祥，对视间，眼神中透露出平和与智慧、眉宇间洋溢着 对知识的渴望与对真理的执着。他们的对话，虽无声却胜有声，每一个眼神的交 换，每一次手势的轻扬，都蕴含着深邃的哲理与无尽的智慧。他们或许在探讨宇 宙的奥秘，或许在传授修行的法门，又或许在分享各自的人生感悟与修行体验。 这份交流，超越了言语的界限，直达观者内心深处。 五彩的双层仰覆式莲台的下装饰着四狮两象，这种布局为典型的帕拉风格元 素（图二）。顺着视线再往下，最下层绘画有十六位手持不同法器，正在舞蹈的天 女，在佛教寓意中，天女们四位一组，共分为四组，分别代表着东南西北四个方 位。唐卡背面绘画有佛塔，佛塔内写有多行藏文，包括以下偈语开头的祈祷 文：Om a hum vajra guru padma siddhi hum，这是莲花生大士的心咒；接下来 是普巴金刚、毗湿奴、文殊菩萨的开光咒；以及观世音菩萨的 Om mani padme hum 六字真言；金刚手菩萨的 Om vajra pani hum ；接下来是“ye dharma hetu...”佛教经文，以及一段《波罗提木叉经》的摘录。随着目光的深入，这幅大尺幅的上师对坐唐卡所营造出的空间感愈发强 烈，如同一幅浓缩的宇宙图卷。它不仅仅局限于画面所展现的物理空间，更通过 两位上师之间的微妙互动，构建了一个超越物质世界的灵性空间。在这个空间 里，时间仿佛失去了意义，外界的喧嚣与纷扰都被隔绝在外，只留下两颗纯净无 染的心灵在相互碰撞、交融。
viewingNote.en: Every time I see those rich, profound colors, i am captivated inevitably. From the deep blue of the sky to the blend of red, white, and black in the buildings, the natural purity and vibrant hues seem to conceal secrets that captivate the soul. Beyond their sensory beauty, these colors evoke a deep sense of awe that stirs within one's heart. This land is mesmerizing, evoking a sense of joy and profound familiarity, as if we have known this place all our lives. I n early civilizations, places brimming with vivid natural colors were seen as havens that enhanced the survival of early humans. Colors were often linked to meanings vital for survival: red, for example, symbolized fire and passion, but also danger and sacrifice, while green, associated with trees and water sources, came to represent peace and life. The colors and shapes of this landscape are intricately woven with human thought and emotion, connecting us to the world around us. This primal resonance with our deepest consciousness fills the land with mystery and vastness. The unique geographical environment and harsh climate create challenging conditions for plant life in Xizang. In the brief periods when growth is possible, life here bursts forth with a vigorous energy, striving to bloom in full, vibrant colors. This fierce display of life imbues the landscape with unmatched enthusiasm and exuberance. The natural scenery, coupled with deep cultural traditions, has forged a distinctive Xizang art form that harmonizes nature and culture, showcasing stunning beauty and visual impact while conveying a spiritual essence rooted in both spirituality and the natural world. When these colors are reflected in art, the historical and cultural context becomes subtly visible, providing a glimpse into the rich tapestry of Xizang heritage. I n 841 AD, the Xizang King Langdarma began persecution and suppression of Buddhism, leading to a period of over 100 years during which Buddhist art disappeared from Xizang. During this time, some monks fled to Amdo (present day Qinghai) became infused with Buddhist influence. Over these centuries, Qinghai gradually developed into a new center for Buddhism. Meanwhile, descendants of the Tubo royal family moved to the Ngari region and established their own dynasties. Buddhism was reintroduced to the U-tsang region from Qinghai and Ngari around 978 AD, setting the stage for the spread of Buddhism through both the Upper and Lower Lineages. I n the 10th century, the rulers of the Guge Dynasty, particularly the third king, Yishe Ö, were ardent patrons of Buddhism. Yishe Ö, known as the most famous protector of Buddhism in Guge's history, actively promoted and supported the religion. He sent 21 members of his family to study Dharma in places like Kashmir. Among them was Rinchen Zangpo, who became a renowned master of sutra translation in the early days of Xizang Buddhism. Upon completing his studies, Rinchen Zangpo returned to Xizang, bringing with him 32 Kashmiri craftsmen to help build temples and create statues, establishing an early art style in western Xizang heavily influenced by Kashmir. I n the 11th century, the arrival of Indian monks such as Dharmapala and Atisha in Xizang marked a new era of Buddhist dissemination. Traveling through Ngari and the U-tsang region, they introduced elements of the Pala style to the heartland of the Xizang plateau. As Buddhism spread throughout Xizang and gained influence, the Pala style, which had become popular in central Xizang, began to permeate western Xizang. This region, initially influenced by Kashmir, saw its artistic traditions evolve through the integration of diverse styles. By the 13th century, with the rise of Nepalese influence, regional artistic expressions blended various aesthetic elements, resulting in unique local artworks. This Thangka of the Kadampa School masters sitting opposite each other is a quintessential example from 13th-century western Xizang, combining elements of Pala and Nepalese aesthetics. The theme of masters, a distinctive art category in Xizang Buddhism, is represented not only in three-dimensional sculptures but also through vibrant colors, lines, and compositions on two-dimensional surfaces. As a bridge between Buddha and sentient beings, the masters enjoy a noble status. After the passing of a master, disciples will sculpt and paint statues and paintings based on his appearance before his death to commemorate him. This portrait painting, with characters as the main theme, depicts the two masters of the Kadampa sect sitting opposite each other. Thangka uses segmented narrative, chess format layout, and a composition form that highlights the theme to depict the scene of communication and dialogue between the two masters during their practice. As the scene unfolds, the first thing that catches our eye is a peaceful yet solemn atmosphere. The vivid, red-dominated composition immediately draws us in. In the upper part of the image, three rows of deities and lamasare arranged in a checkerboard pattern, featuring images of Buddhas, Bodhisattvas, yogis, lamas, and worshippers. These figures represent different realms and powers, showcasing the integrity and richness of Buddhist beliefs while weaving a narrative of inheritance, compassion, and wisdom. As our gaze moves downward, we see ten distinct figures within the cave above the semi-circular backlight, including the Patriarch (or Venerable Atisha), Protector, Buddha, Bodhisattva, and Mahasiddha, all wearing the hats of the Kadampa sect. Among them, Chakrasamvara and Vajrapani are depicted in a typical Yuan Dynasty style, with round and sturdy figures whose chubby forms evoke a sense of joy. Other figures of ancestors and bodhisattvas hold various ritual tools and make different mudras, each symbolizing different Buddhist doctrines through meticulous artistry. Notably, Padampa Sangye, with his bronze-colored skin, is portrayed in his classic image on the right side of the composition, his expression bright, clear, and pure like a polished copper bell. This depiction of Padampa Sangye closely resembles the image found on a 12th-century mural in Pargarbu Caves in western XiThe composition of this thangka is masterful, using spatial segmentation to place the two masters prominently in the center, underscoring their importance. In her 2008 article "Thangka of Two Xizang Teachers of the Kagyu Lineage", Amy Heller believed that this Thangka was inherited from the Kagyu School. Although no inscription definitively identifies the figures, through the analysis of the secular image of the master on the right and his facial features and characteristics seen in other artworks, the author believes that it is more likely Dromton, the founder of the Kadampa School. The portrait on the left side of the picture is likely his teacher or spiritual guide. The two masters are seated together on a lotus platform, which is subtly backlit, with the tail of the platform elegantly morphing into a curling vine, showcasing the influence of Nepalese aesthetics. Although the master’s statue is based on his appearance during his lifetime, the expressions,lines, and brushstrokes between his eyebrows and eyes exude the distinct charm of the Pala painting style. The master’s robes are adorned with floral patterns resembling gold thread embroidery, intricately woven and outlined with precision. Each stroke reveals a sense of rhythm, color, and solemnity. The artist’s devotion and respect are palpable in every detail. The master's face exudes kindness, his gaze imbued with peace and wisdom, and his expression reflecting a thirst for knowledge and a commitment to truth. Their silent conversation, depicted through their eyes and subtle gestures, transcends sound, conveying profound philosophy and boundless wisdom. They may be delving into the mysteries of the universe, teaching methods of cultivation, or sharing personal insights and spiritual experiences. This exchange transcends language, reaching deep into the heart of the observer. The lower lotus petals of the double-layered platform is adorned with four lions and two elephants, a typical element of Pala art (Figure 2). Below this, sixteen celestial maidens are depicted dancing, each holding a different musical instrument. In Buddhist iconography, these maidens represent the four cardinal directions. On the back of the Thangka, there is a drawing of a stupa, with numerous lines of Xizang script inside it. These writings contain prayers beginning with the verse "Om a hum vajra guru padma siddhi hum," the seed mantra for Padmasambhava. This is followed by consecration mantras for Vajrakila, Vagishvara, and Manjushri; "Om mani padme hum" for Avalokiteshvara; "Om vajra pani hum" for Vajrapani; the 'ye dharma hetu...' Buddhist creed; and an excerpt from the Pratimoksa Sutra. As one’s gaze deepens, the sense of space created by the large thangka becomes more pronounced, unfolding like a condensed universe. It transcends the physical dimensions of the painting, creating a spiritual realm beyond the material world through the subtle interplay between the two masters. In this realm, time seems to lose its meaning, and the noise and chaos of the outside world fade away, leaving only the pure and untainted hearts of the masters, deeply connected and harmonizing with each other.

viewingNote.zh: 每次去到巴黎，逛吉美博物馆总是必不可少的行程之一，尽管已经去过很 多次了。每每逛到那几尊喜欢的早期雕塑面前时，我总会停留上好一会儿，那 刻的感受是时间变慢了。整个博物馆仿佛只剩下我与祂之间的静默对视 。我的 思绪被吸引、包裹，心里也逐渐变得安静而满足 。为什么一尊雕塑，会让人的 感知发生如此深刻的变化？祂们静静地站立，不说话、不解释，也不讨好任何 观者的目光 。后知后觉，因为祂们存在于一种比语言更广阔的时间里，那一刻 仿佛不是我在看祂，而是我在通过祂看见自己 。凝视间，疗愈静静发生 ... 第一次看到这件尼泊尔 9-10 世纪的金刚手立像时，接连看了好几天，那种 被吸引的情绪如同逛吉美 。看着这件雕塑我会感觉祂丰富得像一个人，没有任 何单调感，总是给予人一种美好的感受，充满耐看性 。 不同地域的造像，总是悄然携带着它所诞生之地的历史背景与文化记忆 。 李查维王朝是尼泊尔历史上第一个有明确文献记载的王朝，主要势力集中于加 德满都谷地 。由于地理相邻 、文化交汇以及族群联系等多重因素，印度笈多王 朝造像中典雅、内省、充满精神性的艺术风格，对早期尼泊尔李查维时期的造 像产生了深远的影响 。在这尊金刚手菩萨的面容上，我们仍能感受到那种来自 古典时代的纯净气息 。那如呼吸般自然的微笑，仿佛只要多看一眼，心中的浮 躁就能被安抚 。菩萨头戴三叶冠，耳后冠结处点缀着细致的花朵装饰，头顶 发髻高束，如一朵圆形莲蓬；垂肩而下的发丝前方，佩戴着具有明显身份和时 代印记的圆形与花瓣形耳环。 这尊金刚手与其他菩萨造像有所不同的是，大多数时候，我们看到的菩萨 眼神是温柔的、慈悲的，是在凝视着某一个具体的人，回应他的祈愿；而这尊 却相反 。当我试图捕捉祂的眼神时，首先发现的是：在祂的眼里是有着丰富内 容的——那是一种既写实又超然的深度，像是一扇半掩的门，门后通向一个更 辽阔的意识空间。其次，祂的眼神并不在于你，甚至也不是在看任何一个具体 的人 。祂的目光似乎越过了现实与肉身，落在一个遥远而无形的地方。祂如一 位穿越时间的“沉思者”，将万物收于一念之中。那种目光不带评判，也无悲 喜，反而像是在凝望一个永恒的问题，而答案，也并不急着说出，只是缓缓浮 现于那静默之间。这种神韵的无限性所给观者带来的思索和反复回味，也正是 成就了造像耐看性的一个重要因素。 最初第一眼见到这尊金刚手时，首先抓住我眼球并让我开始思索的其实是 祂那带有显著动势的三折姿。因为乍看之下，这样幅度较大的躯干扭动，似乎 会带来某种不稳定感，但令人惊喜的是，细看之下，而祂却呈现出了极强的稳定 性与内在平衡。这种矛盾般的观感，显然是来自匠人对身体结构与视觉张力的 深刻理解与再次创造 。造像整体衣纹线条简练，几乎剔除了冗余的装饰性褶 皱，仅以最基础的线性来勾勒出形体的起伏 。而在身体的块面处理上，也延续 了早期简约的风格，留白与概括并用，使形体在塑造中更具耐看性 。 菩萨肩部宽广、挺阔，一手高抬呈无畏印，给予安心；一手手持象征身份 的金刚杵自然下垂，表征无坚不摧的“金刚之心”。如果我们尝试以造像最中间 的帽冠顶部作为一个点，顺着身体中心绘出一条垂直的中轴线 ，我们就会清晰 的看见匠人在体态塑造方面的创新。右侧上半身形体在空间中的 C 字形运动 轨迹从腋下延展至腰部，再过渡至髋部，形成一个向内扭转的连贯曲线 ，如风 中之枝；下半身腿部则是近乎静止，如一根笔直的立柱般顺延至脚掌垂直而 立，如山岩不动；而左侧上半身没有动态，下半身腿部则是以人体最舒适的姿 势站立，松弛且自然 。于视觉上，左右两侧在动与静之间形成了鲜明的对 照——这正是三折姿之所以“动中有稳”的美学根基 。 可以说，匠人在创作时，并没有过多参照自然人体的动作，而是一种基于真 实生活观察和自己理解之后的“理想化再创作”。匠人并没有拘泥于现实形体 的被动模仿，而是在掌握动态平衡与视觉节奏的基础上，对人体进行了自己的 重构。动静交融之间，不仅凸显了造像的空间张力，也赋予祂一种近乎音乐般 的节奏感，使观者在视觉能捕捉到一种“如行云流水般流动，又如山岳般沉稳” 的双重体验。而这种双重体验也正是成就这尊造像耐看性的另一个重要因 素，也是匠人的高明之处——在看似矛盾中创造和谐，在有限的空间中唤起无 限的生命感 。 我们常说“工艺”决定了外形，但“意蕴”成就了灵魂 。技艺可以雕刻出形，但 意蕴往往来自于匠人的信念、愿力、境界，乃至那个时代的共情。10 世纪的尼 泊尔，随着李查维王朝的结束而进入了混乱的过渡时期，就在那样的时代缝隙 中，这尊金刚手的悄然诞生，似乎并非偶然，应是“另有深意”。祂更像是一种 回应和指引，金刚手眼神中所散发出的美好与希望，也许正是匠人、供养人在 用自己的方式，想把虔诚的观者指引去祂所指的那个方向——平和、自由、超 越 、宽广… 或许，这也是一则关于“抵达”的故事。 那么，你呢？ 你是否，也已经注视中，开始疗愈、开始悄然靠近了那份安定与辽阔？
viewingNote.en: Despite visiting the Guimet Museum many times before, it is still on my checklist every time I go to Paris. Whenever I come across those beloved early sculptures, I linger for a long while— in those moments, time seems to slow down. The entire museum fades away, leaving only silence between me and the sculptures. My thoughts are drawn in, enveloped, and my heart gradually grows still yet full. Why does a single sculpture have the power to evoke such a profound shift in perception? They stand there in silence—not trying to explain anything, seeking no viewer’s approval. Only later do I realize: They exist within a timescale vaster than language. I’m not looking at them, but rather seeing myself through them. In those particular moments, healing unfolds quietly within me... The first time I saw this 9th–10th-century Nepalese standing statue of Vajrapani, I kept admiring it for days, feeling the same magnetic pull as at the Guimet. Gazing at this sculpture, I sensed a richness akin to a living person—no trace of monotony, only an enduring beauty that rewards contemplation. Sculptures from different regions always carry, subtly yet unmistakably, the historical and cultural imprints of their origins. The Licchavi dynasty, the first in Nepalese history with well-documented records, held sway primarily in the Kathmandu Valley. Thanks to geographical proximity, cultural exchange, and ethnic ties, the elegant, introspective, and deeply spiritual artistry of India’s Gupta dynasty profoundly influenced early Licchavi-period sculptures. In the face of this Vajrapani Bodhisattva, one can still detect that classical purity—a smile so natural it feels like breath itself, capable of soothing restless thoughts with just a glance. The Bodhisattva wears a three-leaf crown, adorned with delicate floral motifs where the ribbons meet behind the ears. The hair is swept up into a high topknot, round like a lotus pod, while strands cascade past the shoulders, framing circular and petal-shaped earrings that bear the distinct signature of their era. This Vajrapāṇi statue stands apart from typical bodhisattva imagery in one significant aspect. While most bodhisattvas are depicted with gentle, compassionate eyes—gazing directly at the devotee as if answering a prayer—this one is distinctly different. When I sought to grasp his gaze, I was first struck by its profound depth—at once lifelike and transcendent, like a door left ajar to a boundless realm of awareness. Moreover, his gaze does not settle on you, nor on any one being. It appears to look beyond the physical, resting somewhere distant and intangible. He resembles a meditator moving through time, encompassing all existence within a single thought. His expression holds no judgment, neither joy nor sorrow—only a silent contemplation of something eternal, as though the answer need not be spoken, but slowly reveals itself in the quiet. This spiritual vastness invites repeated reflection, and it is this quality that makes the sculpture endlessly compelling. When I first encountered this statue, my attention was immediately drawn to its strikingly dynamic tribhaṅga posture. Though the pronounced twist of the torso initially suggests a sense of instability, a closer look reveals instead a remarkable inner balance and structural steadiness. This subtle tension between motion and stability arises from the artisan’s deep understanding of anatomy and skillful reinterpretation of form and visual weight. The robes are rendered with concise lines, stripped of almost all superfluous decorative folds, using only the most essential strokes to outline the body’s contours. The treatment of bodily surfaces continues this early minimalist style, employing both blank space and generalization to enhance the figure’s enduring visual appeal. The bodhisattva’s shoulders are broad and upright. One hand is raised in the abhaya mudra, offering reassurance; the other holds a vajra—symbolizing his identity—hanging naturally, representing an indestructible “vajra heart.” If we take the top of the crown as a point and draw a vertical axis along the center of the body, we can clearly see the artisan’s innovation in sculpting the posture. The upper right side of the body traces a C-shaped curve in space, extending from the armpit to the waist and transitioning to the hip, forming a coherent, inward-twisting line like a branch in the wind. The lower body and legs remain nearly still, standing straight as a pillar down to the feet, firm as rock. Meanwhile, the left upper body shows no movement, and the left leg stands in a relaxed, natural pose. Visually, the left and right sides form a vivid contrast between movement and stillness—this is the aesthetic foundation of the tribhaṅga posture’s “stability within motion.” It could be said that the artisan did not simply mimic natural human movement but engaged in an “idealized reinterpretation” based on real-life observation and personal understanding. Not constrained by passive imitation of physical reality, the artisan reconstructed the human form based on an mastery of dynamic balance and visual rhythm. The fusion of movement and stillness not only highlights the statue’s spatial tension but also lends it a nearly musical sense of rhythm, allowing the viewer to visually grasp a dual experience— “flowing like clouds and water, yet steady as a mountain.” This dual experience is another key factor in the statue’ enduring appeal and a testament to the artisan’s brilliance—creating harmony within apparent contradiction, evoking a sense of infinite vitality within limited space. It is often said that “craftsmanship” defines the form, while “meaning” gives life to the soul. Technique may shape the exterior, but significance flows from the artisan’s faith, devotion, inner cultivation, and even the shared spirit of the age. In 10th-century Nepal, as the Licchavi dynasty waned and a period of uncertainty unfolded, the quiet appearance of this Vajrapāṇi in such a transitional time feels deeply meaningful. It serves not only as a response, but as a guide: the beauty and hope reflected in Vajrapāṇi’s gaze may well be how the makers and devotees intended to lead viewers toward what he embodies—peace, freedom, transcendence, boundlessness… Perhaps this, too, is a narrative of “arrival.” And what of you? Have you also, in moments of reflection, started to heal—gently nearing that stillness and expansiveness?

viewingNote.zh: 站在一件雕塑面前，不知道你是否有过这样的体验：在意识纷乱 、情绪噪 杂的时候，目光之间，仅仅一瞥，这件雕塑作品就能穿透混沌，让人大脑放 空、归于平静 ... 那是一个午后，或许是因天气燥热 、或许是琐事堆积，使人心情浮躁不 安。而就在我步入展厅、目光落在这尊造像上的那刻，一种难以描述清楚的力 量悄然发生 。那种感觉不是震撼，而是一种温和的、深入人心的精神气息，让 我从纷乱的意识中抽离，进入一种忘我的观看状态 。我的意识在那一刻并没有 被拉向某个特定的意义，反而是大脑空空如也，情绪也随之沉降，像是忘记了 上一刻也许还在生气，整个人沉入了一种没有烦恼的状态 。 在之后的几天，大脑里不断在回想那一刻被触动的原因。那种感受不是冲 动的，而是一种深入内在、却又难以捕捉的体验 。它不属于理性分析的范 畴，也不只是审美的快感，更像是一种存在的对话——在混乱生活与精神渴望 之间，这件雕塑成为了某种无声的回应体，以一种无为之境，使我有机会重新 照见了自己的内在节奏。 从雕塑角度来说，这是一件尼泊尔 12 世纪有融合帕拉艺术风格的释迦牟 尼造像 。眉弓细长，鼻梁高挺，眼神柔和，嘴角微笑，整个面部情绪带有一种 早期造像特有的沉思和静谧，非常迷人 。身上的衣饰轻薄 、贴体，勾勒出了身 体的线条。从塑造细节来说，无论是从手臂到肩膀再到胸腔的肌肉走向和转 折，塑造写实，而不是概念化的处理，有一种雕塑的美感 。从形象上看，这尊 释迦造像具有一种令人印象深刻的俊朗，很是帅气 。祂就像现实中那个坐在阳 光下、身穿白衬衫的大学男生，干净、清澈、挺拔，是属于年华最美好的阶段 。 这样的形象，带有一种理想化的青春之美 。 释迦一手于胸前结禅定印，一手结触地印，双腿结跏趺座 。腿部修长而开 阔，腿部因弯曲而略显挤压的肌肉，呈现出一种臌胀感与富有弹性的鲜活气 息 。腿部的比例塑造，在视觉上与头部 、肩膀、腰身再到腿部之间构成了稳定 的三角形结构，使整个造像比例舒朗、大气 。这样的构造既体现出神性的庄严 气质，也让整体形象充满了静定的力量。造像下部为仰覆式莲台，宽大的莲瓣 造型简洁有力，具有鲜明的时代特征，与整尊佛像气质和谐统一。 匠人在塑造心中这个理想形象的时候，有考虑到不同角度的观感 。在背部 的塑造，我们可以看到从肩胛骨的起伏，到背部肌肉线条，再到臀部的转折和 层次，轮廓流畅自然，线条变化微妙。即便在整体造型略带概括性的风格 下，依然能看见骨架、肌肉与衣物之间的结构层次 。简约的造像看似容易，实 际是极具挑战的，因为真正的“炫技”，往往隐藏在简约中。 同样，这尊释迦造像的肢体塑造，也令我联想到米开朗基罗的《大卫》，那 种以真实人体为基础，加以理想化的处理方式，使雕塑既具神性之美，也不失 人性的真实与温度 。这尊释迦牟尼像也同样如此——除了庄严神圣的佛性，在 肢体与气质的呈现中，也流露出一种温和的人性美 。 欣赏一件雕塑，就像我们注视一个人 。那种美 、那种情绪，无需言语或反 复确认，它是直接而纯粹地抵达感官与内心的。就像这尊释迦造像，当我第一 眼看到时，祂所传达的情绪是完整而通透的。佛教雕塑的“理想美”，从来都不 是纯形式的炫技，也不只是信仰的情绪，而是来自对生命本质、理想人格的内 在追问，也更是一种关于“最初自我”的记忆 。 就像那个下午，只需一眼对视 ，脚步驻足，整个人就沉入了一种没有烦恼的 状态 。因为我被造像所散发的情绪唤醒了，一种久违的熟悉感袭来 。我忽然 意识到，释迦散发出的青春和理想之美，像是我曾经无数次在人群中、在镜子 里、在某个阳光穿过树影的午后，看到过的神情——属于青春时期那个青 涩、坦白却又明亮的我 。 那个时候，我们懵懂的坐在树下听着卡带机里的“十年”，还不太懂得复杂 世界的运转，却能安心地睡着，以及毫不焦虑地醒来；那个时候，很多的事我 们做不好，说不清，但却不妨碍我们活得真诚 、有趣 、相信未来 。那种生活的 节奏——慢、松、干净、充满余地 。现在我们，早已被成年人的生活节奏替代 。 而那刻，我竟在一尊造像前又重体验到了那种节奏的气息 。祂的“静”是一种 温柔的照见，让我看见那个曾经单纯 、心中有光的自己；那个没有被烦恼层层 裹住、还愿意相信温柔与秩序的青年。 当然，被造像所唤醒的怀念，并不只是对逝去的眷恋，而是一种更深的整 合、一种温柔的提醒——在被现实打磨之后，我们也能用更成熟的方式，靠近 那个理想中的自己 。
viewingNote.en: Have you ever stood before a sculpture and had this experience? Amid the noise of thoughts and emotions, one look at the artwork cuts through the clutter, emptying the mind into stillness… It was an afternoon. I was feeling restless and unsettled, perhaps due to the sweltering heat or the weight of trivial matters. But the moment I entered the exhibition hall and saw this statue, a subtle yet unmistakable presence quietly took hold. It wasn’t awe so much as a deep, gentle resonance that drew me out of my turbulence and into a state of selfless contemplation. In that instant, my mind held no particular meaning—it simply went still. Emotions settled like sediment. The earlier irritations faded, and I sank into a worry-free stillness. In the days that followed, I kept revisiting that moment, trying to grasp what had moved me so deeply. It wasn’t a fleeting impulse but an experience that resonated within, elusive yet profound. It defied rational analysis—it wasn’t just aesthetic pleasure but more like a silent dialogue between the chaos of life and the longing of the spirit. The sculpture became a vessel of wordless response, meeting me in a state of effortless stillness, allowing me to rediscover my inner rhythm. From a sculptural perspective, this is a 12th-century Nepalese Shakyamuni statue blending Pala artistic influences. The elongated brows, high-bridged nose, soft gaze, and faint smile on the lips give the face an early-period contemplative serenity that is utterly captivating. The robes cling lightly to the body, tracing its contours. In terms of craftsmanship, the rendering of muscle structure—from the arms to the shoulders and torso—is lifelike rather than conceptual, embodying a sculptural beauty. Visually, this Shakyamuni exudes a striking handsomeness, like a college student in a white shirt sitting in the sunlight—clean, bright, upright, embodying the most radiant phase of youth. It carries an idealized beauty of adolescence. Shakyamuni’s right hand rests in his lap in the dhyana mudra (meditation gesture), while his left touches the earth in the bhumisparsha mudra (earth-touching gesture). His legs are crossed in full lotus, the elongated thighs slightly compressed by the posture, conveying a sense of supple vitality. The proportions—from the head, shoulders, and waist down to the legs—form a stable triangular composition, giving the statue an open, majestic presence. This structure balances divine solemnity with a grounded, meditative power. The base is a double-lotus pedestal, its broad petals carved with concise strength, a hallmark of the era, harmonizing with the Buddha’s aura. The artisan considered the viewing experience from every angle. The back reveals flowing contours—shoulder blades, the lines of the spine, the subtle transitions of the hips—rendered with natural grace. Even within the somewhat stylized form, the interplay of bone, muscle, and drapery remains structurally nuanced. Simplicity in sculpture is deceptively challenging; true mastery often hides in restraint. The statue’s physicality also brings to mind Michelangelo’s David—an idealized yet humanized form, blending divine beauty with mortal warmth. This Shakyamuni, too, carries not just sacred solemnity but also a gentle humanity in its posture and presence. Appreciating sculpture is like beholding a person. Its beauty, its emotion, needs no explanation—it arrives directly, purely, at the senses and the soul. When I first saw this Shakyamuni, what it conveyed was whole and transparent. The “ideal beauty” of Buddhist sculpture is never mere technique or piety—it arises from an inward search for life’s essence and an idealized vision of being, a memory of one’s “original self.” Just like that afternoon: one glance, one paused moment, and I was immersed in a state free of worry. The statue’s presence awakened something in me—a long-unfelt familiarity. I recognized that the youthful, ideal beauty Shakyamuni embodied resembled expressions I had glimpsed countless times—in crowds, in mirrors, in sunlit hours—expressions that once belonged to my younger self: unguarded, earnest, luminous. But before this statue, I felt it again. Its “stillness” became a tender mirror, reflecting that simpler, lighter self—the youth still free of layered worries, still willing to trust in gentleness and order. What the statue awakened was not mere nostalgia, but a deeper realignment—a soft reminder that even after life’s wear, we can, in more mature ways, return to the selves we once aspired to be.

viewingNote.zh: 这是一尊来自西藏中部的佛造像 。乍看之下，祂似乎与其他很多古代造像 无异，但每次凝视 ，内心总被一种无法言说的情绪悄然触动——像雾、像 风，是一种朦胧的辽阔感，像一种“在”，却又“无形”的存在 。后来我才慢慢 意识到，那是一种“虚空”的感觉 。 这是一尊受到帕拉风格影响的造像 。高高的发髻、细长上扬的眉眼、嘴角 一抹耐人寻味的笑意，以及飞扬的衣袖，都带着一种流动的帕拉艺术风格。那 是千年前从东南边境吹入雪域的风 ，停留在了这尊造像上 。
viewingNote.en: This is a Buddha statue from central Tibet. You may think it is no different from many other ancient Buddhist sculptures, yet each time I look at it, I feel quietly moved by an indescribable emotion—like mist or wind, a presence both hazy and expansive, there yet formless. It took me some time to recognize that what I was sensing was a feeling of “emptiness.” The statue shows clear influences of the Pala style. Its high ushnīṣa, slender upcurved eyes, subtly intriguing smile, and flowing robes all carry the characteristically fluid grace of Pala art. It is as though a wind that blew a thousand years ago from the southeastern borders came to rest in this very sculpture.Since most early statues either had wooden bases or were made without one, we decided to create a base for this piece. I contacted an old friend in Chengdu—Yuren, a master goldsmith we often work with. He has a deep understanding of the “personality” of metal. Whenever we need a base made, he is the first person we turn to. This time was no different.

title.zh: Buddhist Art of the Tibetan Realm, P.110, fig.12.
title.en: Buddhist Art of the Tibetan Realm, p. 110, fig. 12.

viewingNote.zh: 流动、梦幻，让人立刻想到佛教里常说的“空性”。这尊造像，从 第一眼就紧紧抓住了我的视线 。祂的力量带着一种向上迸发的劲 儿，扑面而来 。虽然初看会觉得很柔和，可仔细一感受，又能体会到 其中的攻击性 。这不是矛盾，而是一种刚柔并济的平衡，是一种形 式美本身所具备的张力。 如果从造像的形体入手去观察：高耸的发髻像火焰一般，发髻 上刻着十字金刚杵；头顶五叶概念化塑造的骷颅冠；一手高举金刚 杵，一手捧着颅碗，肩头倚着金刚杖；身上披挂着人头蔓，在虚空中 起舞 。这些特征都指向一个非常鲜明的题材——喜金刚 。祂又被称 为“饮血金刚”，是藏传佛教密宗修习的五大本尊之一，也是无上瑜伽 部的重要本尊。喜金刚的“喜”，指的是“大乐”；而“金刚”则象征 着坚不可摧的智慧与力量。合起来，就是“以不可摧毁的智慧所获得 的究竟大乐”。在萨迦派里，喜金刚几乎是核心本尊，很多修法都围 绕着《喜金刚续》展开；而在噶举、格鲁等派别中，虽然有不同的主 尊，比如胜乐金刚、密集金刚，但喜金刚同样受到修持和尊崇。 在密宗的语境中，舞蹈并不仅仅是身体的动作，而是一种“觉悟 之舞”。它意味着本尊的能量在宇宙与人心之间流转，也意味着智慧 在动态中被传递 。舞蹈的姿态不仅传达力量与活力，更在提醒修行 者：真正的智慧不是静止的，而是在运动、在变化 、在流转中显现出 来的——所谓“动中证空、动中生智”。而祂手中的颅碗 、身上的人头 蔓，则是极其直白的象征：生命本身是无常的，“我”的执念终究要 消解在虚空 。那些人头，并不是为了吓人，而是作为一种提醒——当 我们执着于自我，执着于欲望和身份时，痛苦就会随之而来；而当我 们敢于直面无常，才能真正理解生命的意义 。佛教艺术里的每一个 细节，都不是随意的装饰，而是一种语言。祂的眼睛嵌银，清澈而有穿透力，好像能一下子看穿众生内心 的欲望、破碎与遗憾 。红铜嵌饰的冠叶、嘴唇、项链 ，在视觉上起 到很好的点缀，华丽却毫不庸俗，也彰显了供养人对这尊造像的重 视 。祂的舞姿充满张力，身躯的线条像有弹性的弦，既柔软又充满 爆发力，让人有一种“梦幻”般的观感 。祂单脚站立于支点之上，似 乎随时会腾空而起，但力量却稳稳地从那一点向上生长，像植物破 土而出般蓬勃。这是一种生命向上的冲劲，也体现了匠人对构图美 学的把握 。 雕塑作为一种立体艺术，它的“语言”跟绘画完全不同。绘画 是平面的，是二维的关系；而雕塑是三维空间的，它依靠形体 、比 例、节奏、空间感与质感来构建审美的秩序。一件美的雕塑不只是 “修饰”的堆砌，而是“显现”。它把精神与意义转化为一种可见的形 式，让观看者不仅能看到形，还能感受到背后的精神。尤其在佛教 造像中，这种形式美往往是一种“觉悟之相”的表达，是一种精神永 恒性的呈现 。形式也并不只是表面的“好看”，而是一种深层的符 号——它让人即便不懂佛教的经义，也能在形体中感受到纯粹的“力 量之美”，或者“宁静之美”。 就像这尊喜金刚：祂的线条流动、姿态变幻，让人感觉到祂真 的在虚空中舞动。祂的力量不是藏在内部的，而是直白地具有冲击 性，是扑面而来的。那是一种精神融入形体之后，自然而然散发出 的美 。祂带给我们的感受是一种迷、一种幻，和祂的精神语言极度 契合。那是一种变幻的瞬间性——无常、空性 。而匠人抓住了这个 瞬间，让造像既不僵硬，又气韵充沛。所以，当我们凝视祂时，不仅 仅是在看一尊雕塑，而是在被提醒：一切都是流动的，都是无常的。 而正是在这种流动与幻象中，智慧与觉悟才真正显现 。
viewingNote.en: Fluid, dreamlike—it instantly brings to mind the Buddhist concept of "emptiness." From the very first glance, this sculpture captivated my gaze. Its power carries an upward surge, striking and immediate. While it may appear gentle at first, a closer look reveals an underlying aggression. This is not a contradiction but a balance of strength and softness—a tension inherent in the beauty of form itself. Observing the sculpture’s physical form: the towering crown of hair resembles flames, engraved with a crossed vajra; atop the head rests a conceptualized fiveskull crown; one hand holds a vajra aloft, while the other cradles a skull cup (kapala), with a vajra staff resting against the shoulder; the body is adorned with a garland of human heads, dancing in the void. These features unmistakably point to a distinct subject—Hevajra. Also known as the "Blood-Drinking Vajra," Hevajra is one of the five principal deities in Tibetan Vajrayana practice (Tibetan: Kye dorje) and a key yidam in the Unsurpassed Yoga Tantra. The "joy" in Hevajra refers to "great bliss," while "vajra" symbolizes indestructible wisdom and power. Together, they represent the "ultimate great bliss attained through indestructible wisdom." In the Sakya school, Hevajra is nearly the central deity, with many practices revolving around the Hevajra Tantra. In other schools like Kagyu and Gelug, although different principal deities such as Chakrasamvara or Guhyasamaja are emphasized, Hevajra is equally revered and practiced. In the context of Vajrayana, dance is not merely a physical movement but a "dance of enlightenment." It signifies the flow of the deity’s energy between the cosmos and the human mind, as well as the dynamic transmission of wisdom. The dance posture conveys not only power and vitality but also reminds practitioners that true wisdom is not static—it manifests in motion, change, and flow. This is the idea of "realizing emptiness in movement, generating wisdom in dynamism." The skull cup in the deity’s hand and the garland of human heads are stark symbols: life itself is impermanent, and the attachment to "self" ultimately dissolves into emptiness. Those heads are not meant to frighten but to serve as a reminder— when we cling to the self, to desires and identities, suffering follows; only when we dare to face impermanence can we truly understand the meaning of life. Every detail in Buddhist art is not an arbitrary decoration but a symbol. The deity’s eyes, inlaid with silver, are clear and penetrating, as if they can see through the desires, brokenness, and regrets in the hearts of all beings. The copper-inlaid crown leaves, lips, and necklace serve as elegant visual accents, ornate yet never vulgar, reflecting the devotion of the patron who commissioned this sculpture. The dance posture is full of tension, the lines of the body like elastic strings—soft yet explosive, evoking a dreamlike sensation. Standing on one foot atop a pivot, Hevajra seems poised to leap into the air at any moment. The energy seems to grow steadily upward from that point, vibrant like a plant breaking through the soil. This upward surge of life also demonstrates the artisan’s mastery of compositional aesthetics. As a three-dimensional art form, the aura surrounding a sculpture is entirely different from that of a painting. Paintings are planar, displayed in two dimensions; sculpture exists in three-dimensional space, relying on form, proportion, rhythm, spatial sense, and texture to construct an aesthetic order. A beautiful sculpture is not merely an accumulation of "decoration" but a "manifestation." It transforms spirit and meaning into a visible form, allowing viewers not only to see the shape but also to feel the underlying spirit. Especially in Buddhist sculpture, this beauty of form often expresses an "enlightened appearance," a presentation of spiritual eternality. Form is not just superficial "prettiness" but a deep symbol—it enables even those unfamiliar with Buddhist teachings to feel a pure "beauty of power" or "beauty of tranquility" through the physical form. Just like this Hevajra statue: the flowing lines and dynamic posture make it feel as though the deity is truly dancing in the void. His power is not hidden within but openly impactful, striking, and immediate. It is a beauty that naturally emanates when spirit merges with form. What he evokes in us is a sense of mystery, an illusion, deeply aligned with his spiritual language. It is a momentary transience—impermanence, emptiness. And the artisan captured this moment, making the sculpture neither rigid nor lacking in vitality. Therefore, to gaze upon this Hevajra is not merely to see a sculpture, but to be reminded, everything is momentary, everything is impermanent. Yet it is within this very flow and illusion that true wisdom and enlightenment emerge.

viewingNote.zh: 那烂陀寺，最早可以追溯到公元 5 世纪笈多王朝时期 。说它是寺庙，其实 也不完全准确，它更像是一所大学——而且是世界上最早的、体系化的高等学 府之一。那时候，这里已经有了图书馆、研究机构，还有很成熟的教学体系。 比我们熟知的牛津 、剑桥都早了很多个世纪 。 在寺院最辉煌的时期，有一万多名学生，几千位老师，不光学习佛学，还有 逻辑学、语法学、医学、天文学等等。而且它不是那种“灌输式”教学，反而 更接近我们今天大学里讲的“导师制”，强调深入学习，也注重个人修行。 当然，那烂陀寺的意义远远不只是知识传播。它是当时整个大乘佛教思想 的重要中心，也是世界各地僧侣、学者相互交流的地方。它让东西方的文化在 这里汇流，也让佛教的智慧开始更广泛地传播，影响了中国西藏 、东南亚、汉 传佛教等许多传统 。 我们熟悉的玄奘“西天取经”中那个“西天”，其实指的主要就是印度，而 那烂陀寺，就是他此行最重要的目的地之一。大约公元 627 年，他从长安出 发，经历了 17 年的旅程，最终抵达那里 。在那烂陀寺，他深入学习中观 、瑜伽 行、因明等大乘佛学的核心理论，还在《大唐西域记》中详细记载了寺院的建 筑 、学习氛围和教学体系。他认为，这不仅是佛教的中心，更是当时整个印度 文化思想的代表 。 但历史总有令人沉默的部分。到了公元 1193 年，那烂陀寺遭遇毁灭性的打 击，被异教统治者焚毁 。藏书燃烧了好几个月，僧侣被杀 。寺院不复存在，也 象征着一个文明中心的终结。后来，佛教文化的重心也就逐渐向中国西藏 、日 本等地转移。 文化的断裂，往往也意味着艺术品的毁灭 。于是随着寺院的终结，其造像 也几乎都被埋进了土里，只剩下了少量塔藏和当时被带离开印度的造像，能有 幸得以保留下来 。所以现在我们能在各大公、私馆藏和古董市场中见到的那烂 陀寺造像是及少的。而那烂坨寺由于位于恒河流域 ，所以由其以寺院名称命名 的造像艺术在受到了笈多、秣菟罗、犍陀罗等多种风格的影响后，逐渐发展出了 具有标志性的那烂坨风格。 我们眼前的这尊 9-10 世纪典型的那烂陀寺造像，第一眼给我的感觉是完 整。因为我们能见到的、保存下来了的那烂陀寺造像头顶上的滑盖大多都是丢 失的。其次，是祂在历史中的重要性，已知存留下来的造像数量，放在整个佛 教艺术中，是非常稀少和珍贵的。再就是，可能原来可能窖藏后继续传世的温 润皮壳，有一种时间流转的痕迹 。 观音面容柔和，笑容和煦 。形体并不魁伟，反而显得轻盈而从容，整体给 人一种非常温柔的气息，感觉只要一见到，就会被祂的开心所感染 。观音头戴 宝冠，宝冠顶端为庄严的化佛，再上方是一顶清凉华盖，象征庄严。右手结与 愿印，左手持一朵带着莲茎的莲花，脚踏一朵仿佛从莲座中自然长出的莲 花，姿态挺拔、自在，背后则是一轮圆形背光 。尽管造像的元素很多，但从背 光上就能看出，纹饰都是一些便简单的几何、圆弧、线条为主，所以使得整尊 造像在繁复中并不显笨重，而是呈现出一种轻盈和典雅 。 莲座上是宽厚大片的莲瓣，莲瓣片片相连，这种特征为典型的那烂陀寺风 格，像是一朵绽放的莲花 。莲座前方的莲瓣上刻有梵文题记，这是在那烂陀风 格的造像中少见的一种表现方式 。同样，背光的后面也围绕着形状刻了一圈古 梵文文字，这种书写方式，也同样是那烂陀造像风格惯用的一种表达方式 。 由于造像原来可能是属于窖藏的一种储存环境，皮克会略显斑驳，但经过 后世手心的温度接触，透出了其原本的铜质，会有一种时间感 。每次看到这些 早期的造像，当握在手中时，总会感觉到一种时间的厚重，心总能慢慢沉下 来，像是在提醒：别着急，慢慢来 。 也许是我们现在生活节奏太快了，没时间停下来，所以对时间的感知并不 敏锐；又或者，是那个时代真的离我们太遥远了，以至于我们很容易忽略掉这 些雕塑，见证着我们人类走过了一段非常重要的路。我们今天拥有的一切，不 论是知识、艺术 ，还是信仰、精神，其实都不是凭空出现的。它们是很多很多 代人走过来的结果，是我们和过去之间的一条线 。这些看似沉默的造像，恰恰 告诉我们：我们从哪里来，往哪里去，也许并不需要语言来回答，只需要我们 愿意静下来，去看、去听、去感受 ...
viewingNote.en: Nalanda Monastery dates back to the 5th century during the Gupta Dynasty. It wasn’t merely a monastery in the conventional sense—it was closer to a university, one of the world’s earliest and most systematized institutions of higher learning. It already had libraries, research facilities, and a well-developed educational system in its time. That was many centuries before the rise of Oxford or Cambridge. At its peak, Nalanda hosted over ten thousand students and several thousand teachers. The curriculum extended beyond Buddhism to include logic, grammar, medicine, astronomy, and more. Rather than employing rote learning, the teaching method resembled what we now call a “tutorial system,” emphasizing deep study and personal cultivation. Of course, Nalanda’s significance extended far beyond knowledge dissemination. It was a major center of Mahayana Buddhist thought and a hub where monks and scholars from around the world gathered. It served as a crossroads where Eastern and Western cultures met, allowing Buddhist wisdom to spread more widely and influence traditions in Tibet, Southeast Asia, and Chinese Buddhism. The “Western Heaven” that Xuanzang sought in his famous journey was essentially India. Nalanda was one of his most important destinations. Around 627 CE, he set out from Chang’an and, after 17 years, finally reached the monastery. There, he studied core Mahayana teachings such as Madhyamaka, Yogacara, and logic. He later documented the architecture, academic atmosphere, and educational system of Nalanda in his Great Tang Records on the Western Regions. To him, it was not only the heart of Buddhism but also a symbol of Indian cultural and intellectual life. Yet history also holds moments of silence. In 1193, Nalanda was violently destroyed by foreign rulers. Its libraries burned for months, and many monks were killed. The monastery’s fall marked the end of a great civilizational center. Thereafter, the heart of Buddhist culture gradually shifted to regions like Tibet and Japan. Cultural disruption often means the loss of art. With the monastery’s destruction, most of its statues were buried. Only a few that had been stored in stupas or taken out of India survived. This is why Nalanda sculptures are exceptionally rare in both public and private collections today.Located in the Ganges River region, the artistic style named after Nalanda absorbed influences from Gupta, Mathura, and Gandharan traditions, eventually developing its own distinctive characteristics. This 9th–10th century Nalanda sculpture is first remarkable for its completeness. Unlike most surviving examples that have lost their canopies, this one retains its own. Its historical significance is another key feature, as such works are exceptionally rare and precious. Finally, its surface possesses a warm, time-worn patina—a narrative of its journey preserved through careful handling. The Buddha’s face is gentle, his smile kind. His form is not imposing but light and serene, radiating a soft compassion that feels uplifting at first sight. He wears a jeweled crown topped with a dignified miniature Buddha, above which spreads a cooling canopy—a symbol of sanctity. His right hand forms the varada mudra (gesture of granting wishes), while his left holds a lotus stem in bloom. He stands tall and at ease upon a lotus that seems to grow naturally from the pedestal, framed by a circular halo. Despite the richness of detail, the halo is decorated with simple geometric patterns, arcs, and lines, giving the sculpture a sense of elegance rather than weight. The lotus throne features broad, overlapping petals—a classic Nalanda trait— arranged like a blooming lotus. Rare among Nalanda works, the front petals are inscribed with Sanskrit characters. Similarly, the back of the halo is encircled with ancient Sanskrit script, a customary feature of Nalanda-style iconography. Having likely been stored in a concealed cache, the statue’s surface is slightly mottled, yet repeated handling has polished the original bronze beneath, revealing a palpable sense of time. Every time I hold early sculptures like this, I feel the weight of history, and my heart grows still—as if reminded: don’t rush, take your time. Perhaps our modern lives move too fast to pause and feel the passage of time—or maybe that age is now so distant we easily forget these sculptures once witnessed a vital chapter of human journey. Nothing we cherish today—be it knowledge, art, faith, or spirit—came from void. These are all outcomes of generations of striving, a thread still tying us to the past. These seemingly silent images urge us gently: to know where we come from and where we’re headed asks not for words, but only a willingness to be present—to watch, to listen, and to feel.
